The company last declared a quarterly cash dividend of $0.373 per share, kept unchanged from the prior distribution, and $1.492 per share annualized for a 5.84% dividend yield. I picked up BEPC, the C corporation, as it does not issue a Schedule K-1 tax form. BEP is a limited partnership. Critically, each BEPC share has the same distribution as a BEP unit. However, BEPC is changing hands for $34.17 per share, markedly higher than BEP, which means its dividend yield comes in 147 basis points lower at 4.37%.

Bears would be right to highlight that the yield on the commons and the divergence to the units is now at a material level, possibly capping near-term returns for BEPC on a relative basis. The current gap between both securities is 34%, the gap was 15% the same time a year ago. To be clear, the securities provide the same level of economic interests, but the difference between the tax filing requirements of both assigns one with a greater level of investor angst. More broadly, K-1s can sometimes be received late and place the tax accountability on their individual members rather than the business entity. While I added BEPC to my portfolio, I'm not yet at a full position.

Operations Ramp Up And FFO Growth

BEP generated fiscal 2025 second-quarter revenue of $1.69 billion, up 14.2% over its year-ago comp and a healthy beat by $40 million on consensus estimates. Funds from operations ("FFO") grew to reach $371 million, around $0.56 per unit, growing by 10% per unit over its year-ago comp. Growth during the quarter was driven by the substantial and sustained ramp-up of operational capacity, with 7,700 MW of new development projects reaching commercial operation in the last 12 months. BEP's balance sheet, rated investment grade at "BBB+" by Fitch Ratings, has allowed the company to be aggressively acquisitive. The most recent acquisition was for a 38% stake in Isagen, a Colombian electricity generator and supplier from hydroelectric and thermal power plants. BEP expects the $1 billion transaction to be 2% accretive to 2026 FFO per unit.

BEP is aiming for 10% FFO per unit growth through 2025. The company paid out roughly 67% of second-quarter FFO as a dividend and has compounded its dividend by around 5% annually over the last half-decade. While the C corp offers a dividend yield that's just 13 basis points above the U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y), distributions are set for annual raises. Assuming BEP's valuation stays constant, total returns will come from the aggregate of the dividend yield and annual FFO growth. The units currently trade at 11.4x times its annualized second-quarter FFO, with potential for valuation expansion in the event the Fed cuts rates. Why is this important? BEP held a significant total debt balance as of the end of its second quarter. Corporate borrowings had a 14-year term to maturity, with BEP ending the second quarter with $4.7 billion of liquidity.

Dividend Outlook And Diversification

The company is a remarkable dividend compounder, with the quarterly distributions sustained on an upward trajectory for years. I expect this to continue even against broader headwinds from the U.S. clean energy market as a result of the One Big Beautiful Bill Act ("OBBBA"), President Trump's landmark tax and spend law which will strip investment and production tax credits for U.S. clean energy projects; effectively forming new taxes on the sector of around $7 billion for projects entering service after 2027. BEP is incredibly geographically diversified, with the 47,500 MW of total operating capacity spread across 35 power markets in 25 countries. North America accounts for 48% of total operating capacity, but around 57% of FFO. Critically, BEP's cash flows are highly visible with 90% of generation contracted for an average term of 13 years with roughly 70% of revenues indexed to inflation.

Conclusion

The drill, baby, drill zeitgeist ushered in by the Trump administration has not dented the momentum of BEP's fast-expanding renewables portfolio. The company is guiding to grow FFO per share by at least 10% in 2025 and is set to compound its dividend by a healthy clip in the years ahead. I have a small position in the C corp, but I will look to add to this. The tickers are being rated as a buy.
